fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worked exercises accompanying Notebook: Probability and measure, my notebook on Probability and measure. The conceptual development — definitions, theorems, proofs — lives in the notebook; solved exercises live here, one section per chapter.

html:ol.html:li: A discrete probability space cannot contain an infinite sequence of independent events A_1, A_2, \ldots each with probability 1/2. To see this, denote the event space as \mathcal {X}. Note that P(A_1 \cap A_2) + P(A_1 \cap A_2^c) + P(A_1^c \cap A_2) + P(A_1^c \cap A_2^c) = 1 Therefore, every x \in \mathcal {X} must be in one of those four events and have probability at most 1/4. Similarly, we can construct n disjoint sets in the same manner and bound \mathbb {P}(\{x\}) by 1/n. Taking n \to \infty , we have that \mathbb {P}(\{x\}) = 0. Since \mathcal {X} is a discrete probability space, we must have that \sum _{x \in \mathcal {X}} \mathbb {P}(\{x\}) = 1 However, we have just shown that for each x \in \mathcal {X}, \mathbb {P}(\{x\}) = 0. Thus, we have reached a contradiction.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Let P be a probability measure on a field \mathscr {F}_{0} and for every subset A of \Omega define P^{\star }(A) by (3.1). Denote also by P the extension of P to \mathscr {F}=\sigma (\mathscr {F}_{0}).
Show that A is P^*-measurable if and only if P^*(A) = P_*(A).

html:div.html:p: First suppose that P^*(A) = P_*(A). Let E \subset \Omega . We must show that P^*(E \cap A) + P^*(E \cap A^c) = P^*(E) From part (1) and our hypothesis, \exists \overline {A}, \underline {A} \in \mathscr {F} such that \underline {A} \subset A \subset \overline {A} and P(\overline {A}) = P^*(A) = P_*(A) = P(\underline {A}). Additionally, \exists B \in \mathscr {F} such that E \subset B and P^*(E) = P(B). Then we have that P^*(E \cap A) \leq P^*(B \cap \overline {A}) = P(B \cap \overline {A}) and P^*(B \cap A) \leq P^*(B \cap \underline {A}^c) = P(B \cap \underline {A}^c) Thus, P^*(E \cap A) + P^*(E \cap A^c) \leq P(B \cap \underline {A}^c) + P(B \cap \overline {A}) = P^*(E) as desired.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Remark. I really struggled for a minute on even understanding this problem. The import thing to understand for this problem is what space everything is defined on.
P is defined on the initial field \mathscr {F}_0, which is the closure over finite unions and complements of \Omega .
P^* is defined on the powerset \mathcal {P}(\Omega ). P^* is NOT necessarily a measure on all of these subsets though.
E is NOT necessarily in \mathscr {F} hence the need for B.
P (abuse of notation) is P^* restricted to the P^*-measurable subsets of \Omega . Common question is why we cannot just apply finite additivity and have everything P^*-measurable? The answer is that P^* is not countably subadditive on \mathcal {P}(\Omega ) but only the P^*-measurable sets.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: One self-contained note per book, organized by chapter. Worked exercises live here; the corresponding conceptual notebooks are linked from each manual's preamble.

Worked exercises accompanying Notebook: Reinforcement learning: An introduction, my notebook on Reinforcement learning: An introduction. The conceptual notes live in the notebook; every solved exercise is parked here, one section per chapter.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: In the case of two actions, (assuming that Q_*(a_1) > Q_*(a_2)) the probability of the greedy action being selected is 1 - \epsilon or 0.5.

Following the steps in 2.6,
\begin {align*} Q_{n + 1} &= Q_n + \alpha _n [R_n - Q_n] \\ &= Q_{n - 1} + \alpha _{n - 1}[R_{n - 1} - Q_{n - 1}] + \alpha _n \Big [R_n - (Q_{n - 1} + \alpha _{n - 1}[R_{n - 1} - Q_{n - 1}])\Big ] \\ &\vdots \\ & = \left (\prod _{i = 1}^{n} (1 - \alpha _{i})\right ) Q_1 + \sum _{i = 1}^{n} \alpha _n \left (\prod _{i = 1}^{n} (1 - \alpha _{n - i})\right )R_i \end {align*}

Since all of the arms in the testbed have distributions with support less than 5, in the beginning the unpulled arm's optimistic estimates will be higher than the empirical estimates of the previously pulled arms. Therefore, in the early stages all of the arms will be pulled in a round robin fashion, and assuming deterministic rewards the optimal arm will keep being pulled until it falls below the other optimistic values.

Since there are 10 arms (presumably with the same initialization), the UCB bonus will enforce that the first 10 pulls are round robin (uniform) pulling of each of the 10 arms. Then on the 11th step each will have the same UCB bonus term but the one with the highest empirical reward will be pulled, leading to a spike. It then drops in subsequent steps because the UCB bonus of that arm will decrease and other (possibly less optimal) arms are pulled in subsequent steps.

I think you draw the line at where you can already execute the necessary behavior to get from one state to another. This is eerily familiar to the line of work known as hierarchical reinforcement learning, where you gradually learn higher level of abstraction by executing subpolicies.

You have not effectively communicated the task. The agent has no incentive to solve the maze as fast as possible. This means that no matter the duration it took to solve the task the agent would receive the same reward. To fix this, you can give -1 reward at each time step, or use a discount factor to capture the time.

Due to the recursive relationship, we will start from the back
\begin {align*} G_5 &= 0 \\ G_4 &= R_{5} + \gamma G_5 = 2 \\ G_3 &= R_{4} + \gamma G_4 = 4 \\ G_2 &= R_{3} + \gamma G_3 = 8 \\ G_1 &= R_{2} + \gamma G_2 = 6 \\ G_0 &= R_{1} + \gamma G_1 = 2 \end {align*}

In the episodic case, this would not be the same. In the maze running example, v_c would be larger for longer episodes. This would then incentivize the agent to actually take LONGER, where as we want the agent to solve the maze as fast as possible.
This is similar to Exercise 3.12. Namely, v_{\pi }(s) = \mathbb {E}_{\pi }[q_{\pi }(s, a)] = \sum _{a} \pi (a \mid s) q_{\pi }(s, a)

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Review the methods discussed in the first part of the book. What are their strengths and weaknesses? When should they be used?

html:tr.html:td: Simplest case. Can isolate exploration problem.
No credit assignment. Action only effect next timestep.
Clinical trials. Things without temporally extended outcomes.
Complexity blows up with state and action space. Model-based.
Extremely high variance. Must wait till end of episode.
When interacting with environment is inexpensive, episode length is short.
Environment interaction is cheap but also episode length is long or continuing.
When interacting with environment is costly i.e. driving.

fr:title.text: Planning and Learning with Tabular Methods

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Planning and Learning with Tabular Methods

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: If you pick n large enough, then the "first pass" would be just as good. This means that you will be able to update the Q-values of every (s,a) pair you encountered. However, with Dyna you are updating your Q-value based on updated Q-values, meaning that you are no longer limited to the n-step trajectory encountered. Thus, I do not think that n-step methods can match Dyna with a perfect world model.

You could modify the algorithm by maintaining an empirical distribution for states and rewards in your model instead of the exact deterministic transition. To handle changing environments, you could then weight the updates to the empirical distribution to value the more recent entries higher i.e. \hat {R}_{t + 1}(S,A) = \lambda \hat {R}_{t}(S, A) + (1 - \lambda )R_{t + 1}(S, A)

This would strengthen the case for sample updates because the samples would most likely concentrate around the higher probability states. Since these states are much more likely to occur, the computed Q-values will likely end up very similar without the extra computation required by the expected update.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Show that tabular methods are a special case of linear function approximation. What would the feature vectors be?

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Solution.
As a linear function approximation method, we have two quantities \boldsymbol {w} and \boldsymbol {x}(s). We make the update w_{t + 1} = w_{t} + \alpha (r_t + \gamma w_{t}^{\top } x_{t+1} - w_t^{\top } x_t) x_t In the tabular setting, we just assume that we have the capacity to represent every possible state. This means that x(s) \in \mathbb {R}^{|S|}. Therefore, if we define the features as x : s_i \mapsto \boldsymbol {e}_i then we can recover policy evaluation by taking, r_i = \mathbb {E}_{a \sim \pi , s \sim p}[r(s_i)], \quad w_i = v_{\pi }(s_i) Substituting, we get
w_{t + 1} = w_{t} + \alpha (\mathbb {E}_{a \sim \pi , s \sim p}[r(s_i)] + v(s_{t + 1}) - v(s_{t})) \cdot \boldsymbol {e}_i
Since \boldsymbol {w} is our vector of values, updating the ith entry is exactly performing exactly one update to v_{\pi }(s_i) in the tabular setting.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Why does (9.17) define (n + 1)^k distinct features for dimension k?

html:div.html:p: For each s_j, there are n + 1 options for c_{i, j}. Since there are k, s_{j}'s, there are (n + 1)^k total possible features for x_i.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: You could do anisotropic (big word for asymmetric) tile partitioning. If we consider the two state dimensions as (x,y) coordinates and suppose that we want to only generalize across the y-direction i.e. we want states with the same x coordinate to have similar values then we would tile with long thin tiles. Therefore, states with the same x coordinate would lie in the same vertical tile and if the tiles are very thin any change in x-coordinate would lie in disjoint tiles.

Worked exercises accompanying Notebook: Probability and measure, my notebook on Probability and measure. The conceptual development — definitions, theorems, proofs — lives in the notebook; solved exercises live here, one section per chapter.

html:ol.html:li: A discrete probability space cannot contain an infinite sequence of independent events A_1, A_2, \ldots each with probability 1/2. To see this, denote the event space as \mathcal {X}. Note that P(A_1 \cap A_2) + P(A_1 \cap A_2^c) + P(A_1^c \cap A_2) + P(A_1^c \cap A_2^c) = 1 Therefore, every x \in \mathcal {X} must be in one of those four events and have probability at most 1/4. Similarly, we can construct n disjoint sets in the same manner and bound \mathbb {P}(\{x\}) by 1/n. Taking n \to \infty , we have that \mathbb {P}(\{x\}) = 0. Since \mathcal {X} is a discrete probability space, we must have that \sum _{x \in \mathcal {X}} \mathbb {P}(\{x\}) = 1 However, we have just shown that for each x \in \mathcal {X}, \mathbb {P}(\{x\}) = 0. Thus, we have reached a contradiction.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Let P be a probability measure on a field \mathscr {F}_{0} and for every subset A of \Omega define P^{\star }(A) by (3.1). Denote also by P the extension of P to \mathscr {F}=\sigma (\mathscr {F}_{0}).
Show that A is P^*-measurable if and only if P^*(A) = P_*(A).

html:div.html:p: First suppose that P^*(A) = P_*(A). Let E \subset \Omega . We must show that P^*(E \cap A) + P^*(E \cap A^c) = P^*(E) From part (1) and our hypothesis, \exists \overline {A}, \underline {A} \in \mathscr {F} such that \underline {A} \subset A \subset \overline {A} and P(\overline {A}) = P^*(A) = P_*(A) = P(\underline {A}). Additionally, \exists B \in \mathscr {F} such that E \subset B and P^*(E) = P(B). Then we have that P^*(E \cap A) \leq P^*(B \cap \overline {A}) = P(B \cap \overline {A}) and P^*(B \cap A) \leq P^*(B \cap \underline {A}^c) = P(B \cap \underline {A}^c) Thus, P^*(E \cap A) + P^*(E \cap A^c) \leq P(B \cap \underline {A}^c) + P(B \cap \overline {A}) = P^*(E) as desired.

Remark. I really struggled for a minute on even understanding this problem. The import thing to understand for this problem is what space everything is defined on.
P is defined on the initial field \mathscr {F}_0, which is the closure over finite unions and complements of \Omega .
P^* is defined on the powerset \mathcal {P}(\Omega ). P^* is NOT necessarily a measure on all of these subsets though.
E is NOT necessarily in \mathscr {F} hence the need for B.
P (abuse of notation) is P^* restricted to the P^*-measurable subsets of \Omega . Common question is why we cannot just apply finite additivity and have everything P^*-measurable? The answer is that P^* is not countably subadditive on \mathcal {P}(\Omega ) but only the P^*-measurable sets.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Worked homework for Three Easy Pieces. One section per chapter; the conceptual material for each chapter lives in the corresponding concept pages — Processes Intro across Process, Process API, Process control block; Process Interlude across fork(), wait(), exec(), Unix signals.

html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads does SJF scheduling deliver the same turnaround as FIFO scheduling?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. For workloads that arrive in increasing order of total job time.
html:ol.html:li: For what types of workloads and quantum lengths does SJF deliver the same response times as Round-robin scheduling?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. For shorter jobs and longer quantum lengths, RR and SJF should deliver similar response times.
html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with SJF as job lengths increase?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Response time degrades because the longest jobs have to wait for each of the shorter jobs to finish. If these shorter jobs become longer than the response time grows linearly.
html:ol.html:li: What happens to response time with RR as quantum lengths increase? Can you write an equation that gives the worst-case response time, given N jobs?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. The response time is the average over stride length number of preceding jobs. The formula can be written as \mathrm {RT} = \frac {1}{N} \sum _{i = 1}^{N} (i - 1)S = \frac {S(N - 1) N}{N} = S(N - 1)
html:ol.html:li:
Run process.py with the flags -l 5:100, 5:100. What should the CPU utilization be?
Answer. The flags correspond to running two processes each with 5 instructions where each instruction has probability one of using the CPU. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu cpu with this, we can conclude that the utilization is 100%. We will execute one instruction for the first process until it completes, then the five instructions of the next process. If both are waiting on IO, then I do not think this would be the case?
Solution. Using the solution flags -cp, there is 100% utilization.

Run process.py with the flags -l 4:100, 1:0. How long does it take to complete both processes?
Answer. The flags correspond to running one process with 4 instructions with each instruction having probability one of using the CPU and one process with one instruction that uses the IO. The output from the program is Process 0 cpu cpu cpu cpu
Process 1 io io_done It will take 4 + \text {time of IO}. Since the process with 4 non-IO-dependent instructions will run each of those instructions and then the next process will begin which must wait for the entire duration of IO.
Solution. Using the -cp flags it takes 11 timesteps to complete which is \approx 4 + \text {time of IO}.

Now switch the order of the processes: ./process-run.py -l 1:0,4:100. What happens now? Does switching the order mat- ter? Why?
Answer. The output of the code is Process 0 io io_done
Process 1 cpu cpu cpu cpu Depending on the time of IO (the -L flag), it should take \max (5, 1 + \text {time IO}). Switching the order does matter because we can run the CPU instructions while waiting for non-blocking IO. This motivates the idea of scheduling.
Solution. It only takes 7 timesteps because we are able to perform the IO operations off the CPU as expected.

We'll now explore some of the other flags. One important flag is -S, which determines how the system reacts when a process issues an I/O. With the flag set to SWITCH_ON_END, the system will NOT switch to another process while one is doing I/O, instead waiting until the process is completely finished. What happens when you run the following two processes, one doing I/O and the other doing CPU work? (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH_ON_END)
Answer. The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED READY 1 3 BLOCKED READY 1 4 BLOCKED READY 1 5 BLOCKED READY 1 6 BLOCKED READY 1 7* RUN:io_done READY 1 8 DONE RUN:cpu 1 9 DONE RUN:cpu 1 10 DONE RUN:cpu 1 11 DONE RUN:cpu 1 As expected, it runs the IO process, blocking the CPU process until it is completed.

Now, run the same processes, but with the switching behavior set to switch to another process whenever one is WAITING for I/O (-l 1:0,4:100 -c -S SWITCH ON IO). What happens now? Use -c and -p to confirm that you are right
html:ol.html:div: Answer. I assume that at timestep 1 we will still RUN:io. However, we will then switch the PID: 1 for the next 4 timesteps until it is completed. After one additional BLOCKED timestep, we will run RUN:io_done and complete both processes.

Solution. The output of the code is: Time PID: 0 PID: 1 CPU IOs 1 RUN:io READY 1 2 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 3 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 4 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 5 BLOCKED RUN:cpu 1 1 6 BLOCKED DONE 1 7* RUN:io_done DONE 1
html:ol.html:li:
One other important behavior is what to do when an I/O completes. With -I IO RUN LATER, when an I/O completes, the process that issued it is not necessarily run right away; rather, whatever was running at the time keeps running. What happens when you run this combination of processes? (./process-run.py -l 3:0,5:100,5:100,5:100 -S SWITCH ON IO -I IO RUN LATER -c -p) Are system resources being effectively utilized?
Answer. The resources are not efficiently utilized because we end up waiting until all of our CPU-bound processes finish before returning to the IO-bound process, which calls another IO:run and we do not get any concurrency benefit.
Solution. The code output is pretty long but the end output is Stats: Total Time 31 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (67.74%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (48.39%)

Now run the same processes, but with -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE set, which immediately runs the process that issued the I/O. How does this behavior differ? Why might running a process that just completed an I/O again be a good idea?
Answer. The behavior differs by returning to the IO-bound process, which can then immediately call the next IO:run before returning access to the CPU-bound processes, improving concurrency. In an attempt to predict the next IO call, it might be a good idea to run the process that just completed IO because it is more likely to make another IO call.
Solution. The corresponding statistics are Stats: Total Time 21 Stats: CPU Busy 21 (100.00%) Stats: IO Busy 15 (71.43%)

Now run with some randomly generated processes, e.g., -s 1 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 2 -l 3:50,3:50, -s 3 -l 3:50,3:50. See if you can predict how the trace will turn out. What happens when you use -I IO RUN IMMEDIATE vs. -I IO RUN LATER? What hap- pens when you use -S SWITCH ON IO vs. -S SWITCH ON END?
Answer. Since each instruction has a 50% chance of being CPU or I/O, I do not think there will be much benefit to IO_RUN_LATER vs. IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE. However, I believe it will be crucial to enable -S SWITCH_ON_IO over -S SWITCH_ON_END as the number of processes grow because we should always have waiting CPU instructions to run. In the case of two processes, we still may not improve that much because they will likely both be IO-bound at some point.
Solution. For -s 1 (aka the seed), the observed performance between IO_RUN_IMMEDIATE and IO_RUN_LATER is the same. However, we do observe an improvement on both IO and CPU usage when we enable SWITCH_ON_IO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberate-Practice (6/66).

html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork(). Before calling fork(), have the main process access a variable (e.g., x) and set its value to something (e.g., 100). What value is the variable in the child process? What happens to the variable when both the child and parent change the value of x
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Both the parent and the child get a copy of the variable values i.e. x. When they modify x it only affects the copy of x in their local address space.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that opens a file (with the open() system call) and then calls fork() to create a new process. Can both the child and parent access the file descriptor returned by open()? What happens when they are writing to the file concurrently, i.e., at the same time?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Both do have access to the same file descriptor. I believe that they can interleave writes if writing at the same time but for me whichever process got ran first always finished writing prior the other starting. This may be a scheduling thing, where one process is able to complete before the other is scheduled?
html:ol.html:li: Write another program using fork(). The child process should print "hello"; the parent process should print "goodbye". You should try to ensure that the child process always prints first; can you do this without calling wait() in the parent?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Using the previous problem, in the child, we can write something to a file. Then, in the parent, we can enforce an ordering by making the parent wait in a while loop until it reads the value written by the child. However, this is terribly inconvenient (and probably why wait() exists).
html:ol.html:li: Now write a program that uses wait() to wait for the child process to finish in the parent. What does wait() return? What happens if you use wait() in the child?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. wait() returns the pid of the child process that died. Using wait() in the child just immediately returns -1.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that calls fork() and then calls some form of exec() to run the program /bin/ls. See if you can try all of the variants of exec(), including execl(), execle(), execlp(), execv(), execvp(), and execvP(). Why do you think there are so many variants of the same basic call?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. It seems that some of them support different types of arguments i.e. execv family allows variable size argument list. Additionally, there is a distinction in the environment. This seems to be a list of environment variable values that exec{l,v}e() will search over. The p family use the same search as the shell i.e. the PATH.
html:ol.html:li: Write a slight modification of the previous program, this time using waitpid() instead of wait(). When would waitpid() be useful?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. If a parent process has multiple children, then wait() will only wait until the first one dies. waitpid() lets you specify waiting on a specific child process.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates a child process, and then in the child closes standard output (STDOUT_FILENO). What happens if the child calls printf() to print some output after closing the descriptor?
html:ol.html:div: Answer. Nothing prints in the child process. However, stdout is still open in the parent process and able to print.
html:ol.html:li: Write a program that creates two children, and connects the standard output of one to the standard input of the other, using the pipe() system call
html:ol.html:div: Answer. This required fflush() for me. I am not sure if this is true in general?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For these notes, I will attempt to refresh my knowledge of measure-theoretic probability along with learning stochastic processes for the first time. I will be following Probability and Stochastics by Cinlar. Unfortunately, there are no solutions to Exercises, but it is by far the best written book I have found.
The worked exercises are collected separately in Billingsley Probability and Measure: Exercises. What stays here is the conceptual development.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
20241120https://kellenkanarios.com/000S/000S/000S/Additivity of IntervalsTheorem
We have the following properties
If \bigcup _{k}I_{k}\subset I, and the I_{k} are disjoint, then \sum _{k}|I_{k}|\leq |I|.
If I\subset \bigcup _{k}I_{k} (the I_{k} need not be disjoint), then |I|\leq \sum _{k}|I_{k}|.
If I=\bigcup _{k}I_{k}, and the I_{k} are disjoint, then |I|=\sum _{k}|I_{k}|.
Remark. The important takeaway from Additivity of Intervals is that extending (2) from finite to countable unions requires Heine-Borel theorem, illustrating that this extension is non-trivial. This will be important to remember as we continue to extend the Lebesgue measure to \sigma -algebras.
This section aims to motivate why we need to approach probability from a measure theoretic point of view. As a concrete example, Dyadic Intervals sets up the dyadic-interval representation of an infinite coin toss, and Exercise 1.1 makes precise why no discrete probability space can carry that sequence. 20241114https://kellenkanarios.com/000L/000L/000L/Dyadic IntervalsExample
Represent coin toss as dyadic expansion consider the set\left [\omega :\ d_{i}(\omega )=u_{i},\,i=1,\ldots ,n \right ]=\Big (\sum _{i=1}^{n} \frac {u_{i}}{2^{i}},\ \sum _{i=1}^{n}\frac {u_{i}}{2^{i}} + \frac {1}{2^{n}}\Big ).
More stuff I think my main takeaway from this is that infinite sequences break discrete probability because each discrete event will have probability zero. This is not actually a problem because in an infinite sequence the occurence of any such sequence "feasibly" has zero probability. However, we still want to reason about probabilistic things like the convergence of the average of the sequence. Since discrete probability is broken, we cannot even use it for these things. This is why we need to use measure theory to allow for zero probability sequences, but still provide information about these more "general" events. Who knows if this intuition is right though?

html:ul.html:li: After a bit more digging, the main culprit here is the countable subadditivity condition of measure.

In a discrete probability space, the entire space is at most countable. Therefore, if each individual event has zero probability then the countable union i.e. the whole space will have zero probability.
In measure theoretic probability, we are able to accumulate non-zero probability mass from an uncountable union of zero measure events.
I guess the canonical example of this is that each point on a line has length 0, but the line itself (an uncountable union of points) has non-zero length.
In the dyadic intervals example, each event of an arbitrary discrete probability space can be identified with a point on the line of probability zero.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It seems, in this section, we aim to answer why certain decisions were made regarding what can be analyzed. For example,
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Why do we only allow consider countable unions in the definition of a \sigma -field?
Answer. Because this is what god intended.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If \mathscr {F} is a \sigma -field in \Omega and P is a probability measure on \mathscr {F}, the triple (\Omega ,\mathscr {F},P) is called a probability measure space, or simply a probability space. A support of P is any \mathscr {F}-set A for which P(A)=1.

fr:title.text: Continuity and countable subadditivity of a probability measure

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Continuity and countable subadditivity of a probability measure

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let P be a probability measure on a field \mathscr {F}.
Continuity from below: If A_{n} and A lie in \mathscr {F} and \mathscr {A}_{n}\uparrow A, then P(A_{n})\uparrow P(A).
Continuity from above: If A_{n} and A lie in \mathscr {F} and A_{n}\downarrow A, then P(A_{n})\downarrow P(A).
Countable subadditivity: If A_{1},A_{2},\ldots and \bigcup _{k=1}^{\infty }A_{k} lie in \mathscr {F} (the A_{k} need not be disjoint), then
P\left (\bigcup _{k=1}^{\infty }A_{k}\right )\leq \sum _{k=1}^{\infty }P(A_{k})

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Here we will see how we can define a probability measure on an algebra \mathscr {F}_0 and get a unique extension to the \sigma -field \mathscr {F} = \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0). I guess the key here is uniqueness? 20241121https://kellenkanarios.com/000T/000T/000T/Outer MeasureDefinition
For each subset A of \Omega , P^{*}(A)=\mathrm {inf}\,\sum _{n}P(A_{n}), where A \subset \bigcup _{n = 1}^{\infty } A_n.
Remark. Taking P_*(A) = 1 - P^*(A^c) gives the inner measure. This is just analogously approximating from the inside.
Intuition is that the sets that are "measurable" should be sets that can be arbitrarily approximated from the inside and outside, or equivalently P^*(A) + P^*(A^c) = 1
My intution for this is that if a set is not arbitrarily well approximated by countable other sets from our algebra then we cannot hope to get an accurate measure from our measure defined on only the algebra.
The countableness is just from the definition of the \sigma -field.
Then we can use P^* as our measure. To do this, we will need to show that P^* is countable additive as one might expect. The rest of the properties of a Probability Measure are trivial.
Proof.
Suppose that A \subset \bigcup _{n = 1}^{\infty } A_n. By the definition of Outer Measure, we can find some B_{nk}, such that A_n \subset \bigcup _{k = 1}^{\infty } B_{nk} where \sum _{k} P(B_{nk}) < P^*(A_n) + \epsilon 2^{-k} Now take C_n = \bigcup _{k} B_{nk}, so that \bigcup _{n} A_n \subset \bigcup _{n} C_n and P^*\left (\bigcup _n A_n\right ) < \sum _{n} P(C_n) = \sum _{n} \sum _{k} P(B_{nk}) < \sum _{n} P^*(A_n) + \epsilon Since \bigcup _{n} A_n = A, we have achieved the desired result.
It turns out that for some reason only enforcing A and its complement to sum to 1 is not restrictive enough? From this, we end up with the final condition
20241121https://kellenkanarios.com/000U/000U/000U/P^*-measurableDefinition
A is P^*-measurable if for every E \subset \Omega , we have that P^*(A \cap E) + P^*(A^c \cap E) = P^*(E)
You then only need to show two things:
The class of P^*-measurable sets contains \mathscr {F_0},
The class of P^*-measurable sets is a \sigma -field. From these, we can conclude that P^* is at least defined on a \sigma -field containing \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0). Restricting P^* to \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0), then yields the desired extension.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
We now want to show that the extension we developed in the previous section is actually unique. The first question we have is what do we mean by unique? To answer this, we need to remember what we have done so far.
We have a Probability Measure P, defined on an algebra \mathscr {F}_0.
We then extended P to \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0) via P^*, so that for every A \in \mathscr {F}_0 we have P(A) = P^*(A) and P^* is defined on all of \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0) From this, our only restriction is that P^*(A) = P(A) for A \in \mathscr {F}_0. Therefore, uniqueness here means that if P^*(A) = Q(A) for some Q and every A \in \mathscr {F}_0. Then P^*(A) = Q(A) for every A \in \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0).
A class \mathcal {P} of subsets of \Omega is a \pi -system if it is closed under the formation of finite intersections: A,B\in \mathcal {P} \implies A\cap B\in \mathcal {P}.
I was a bit unsure that a \lambda -system is not a \sigma -field, but the author provided an example that I will walk through to understand the distinction
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Consider a four point space \Omega = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}. We can define a \lambda -system by taking \mathscr {L} = \emptyset , \Omega , and the six two point sets. The union of any two disjoint two point sets will be \Omega . However, a non-disjoint union of two disjoint sets can result in a three point set, which is not in \mathscr {L}. These non-disjoint unions would be covered in a \sigma -field.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: To remedy this disconnect, we have the following lemma:

fr:title.text: A \pi -system that is also a \lambda -system is a \sigma -field

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A \pi -system that is also a \lambda -system is a \sigma -field

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A class that is both a \pi -system and a \lambda -system is a \sigma -field.
In our example, we could take the intersection of say \{x_1, x_2\} and \{x_2, x_3\} to get \{x_2\}. We could then take a disjoint union of \{x_1, x_3\} and \{x_2\} to get \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}.
f \mathscr {P} is a \pi -system and \mathscr {L} is a \lambda -system, then \mathscr {P}\subset \mathscr {L} implies \sigma (\mathscr {P})\subset \mathscr {L}.
Now we will see how this allows us to prove our desired result i.e. the uniqueness of our extension of a probability on an algebra via the outer measure.

Suppose that P_{1} and P_{2} are probability measures on \sigma (\mathscr {P}), where \mathscr {P} is a \pi -system. If P_{1} and P_{2} agree on \mathscr {P}, then they agree on \sigma (\mathscr {P}).
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Proof. Take \mathscr {L} to be the class of sets such that A \in \mathscr {L} \implies P_1(A) = P_2(A). Since \mathscr {P} is a \pi -system, from Theorem 9, it is sufficient to show that \mathscr {L} is a \lambda -system. Since \Omega \in \mathscr {P}, \Omega \in \mathscr {L}. If A \in \mathscr {L}, then P_1(A^c) = 1 - P_1(A) = 1 - P_2(A) = P_2(A^c). Therefore, A^c \in \mathscr {L}. Finally, if A_1, A_2, \ldots disjoint then
P_1\left (\bigcup _{n = 1} A_n\right ) = \sum _{n = 1} P_1(A_n) = \sum _{n = 1}P_2 (A_n) = P_2\left (\bigcup _{n = 1}A_n\right )
Thus, \mathscr {L} is a \lambda -system. From Theorem 9, \sigma (\mathscr {P}) \subset \mathscr {L} and P_1 and P_2 agree on all of \sigma (\mathscr {P}).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An (important?) note is that this works due to our definition of a Probability Measure. Namely, countable additivity is really a condition on countable disjoint unions, meaning that the \sigma -field induced by a Probability Measure does not make full use of the generality allowed under arbitrary countable unions because it must satisfy countable additivity.
For completeness and future use, we introduce here the notion of monotone classes and Halmo's useful theorem.
If \mathscr {F}_{0} is a field and \mathscr {A} is a monotone class, then \mathscr {F}_{0}\subset \mathscr {A} implies \sigma (\mathscr {F}_{0})\subset \mathscr {A}.
Also for completeness, we introduce completeness lol.
A probability measure (\Omega , \mathcal {F}, P) is complete if for A \subset B if P(B) = 0 then P(A) = 0.
You might be wondering when this is not the case because we have subadditivity. However, it is not the case that P(A) > 0 but instead that A is not measurable. As an example,

If we consider [0,1], then we can partition [0, 1] into equivalent classes as x \sim y if x - y = r for some r \in \mathbb {Q} \cap [0, 1]. Denote each of these as V_{r}. By the Axiom of Choice, we can choose one representative from each V_r. We call the set of these representatives V. Therefore, we can write [0, 1] as a countable union via [0,1] = \bigcup _{r \in \mathbb {Q}} V + r. For the Lebesgue measure \lambda , each \lambda (V + r) = \lambda (V). Since each V + r is disjoint, we have \lambda \left (\bigcup _{r \in \mathbb {Q}} V + r\right ) = \sum _{r \in \mathbb {Q}} \lambda (V) If \lambda (V) = 0, then \lambda ([0,1]) \leq \lambda (V) = 0 which is a contradiction. Otherwise \lambda ([0,1]) = \sum _{r \in \mathbb {Q}} k = \infty for some k \in \mathbb {R}. Thus, V is not Lebesgue measurable.
Exercise 3.2 makes the characterization sharp: a set is P^*-measurable iff its outer and inner measures agree.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The results of this section concern infinite sequences of events in a probability space. They will be illustrated by examples in the unit interval. By this will always be meant the triple (\Omega , \mathscr {F}, P) for which \Omega is (0,1), \mathscr {F} is the \sigma -field \mathscr {B} of Borel sets there, and P(A) is for A in \mathscr {F} the Lebesgue measure \lambda (A) of A. 2024122https://kellenkanarios.com/001J/001J/001J/Conditional ProbabilityDefinition
P(A | B) = \frac {P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} 2024122https://kellenkanarios.com/001I/001I/001I/Chain Rule of ProbabilityProposition
P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A)P(B | A)P(C | B \cap A) 20241226https://kellenkanarios.com/001M/001M/001M/limsup of setsDefinition
For a sequence A_1, A_2, \ldots of sets, we define \lim \sup _{n} A_n = \bigcap _{n = 1}^{\infty } \bigcup _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k In english, an element \omega is in \lim \sup _{n} A_{n} if \omega is in A_{n} infinitely often.
20241226https://kellenkanarios.com/001N/001N/001N/liminf of setsDefinition
For a sequence A_1, A_2, \ldots of sets, we define \lim \inf _{n} A_n = \bigcup _{n = 1}^{\infty } \bigcap _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k In english, an element \omega is in \lim \inf _{n} A_{n} if \omega is in all but finitely many A_{n}.
2024122https://kellenkanarios.com/001K/001K/001K/Fatou's lemma for eventsTheorem
For each sequence \{A_{n}\},
P\left (\lim \inf _{n}A_{n}\right )\leq \lim _{n}\inf _{n}P\left (\,A_{n}\right ) \leq \lim _{n}\sup _{n}P\left (\,A_{n}\right )\leq P\left (\lim _{n}\sup _{n}A_{n}\right ).
Proof. We define B_n \coloneqq \bigcup _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k and B_n as C_n = \bigcap _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k. Note that \{B_n\} is a monotonically decreasing sequence to \lim \sup _n A_n, and C_n monotonically increases to \lim \inf _{n} A_n. Therefore, we can apply continuity from below / above such that
\begin {align*} \inf _n P(A_n) \geq P(C_n) \iff \lim _n \inf _n P(A_n) &\geq \lim _n P(C_n) \\ &= P(\lim _n C_n) \\ &= P(\lim \inf _n A_n) \end {align*}
Similarly,
\begin {align*} \sup _n P(A_n) \leq P(B_n) \iff \lim _n \sup _n P(A_n) &\leq \lim _n P(B_n) \\ &= P(\lim _n B_n) \\ &= P(\lim \sup _n A_n) \end {align*}
20241226https://kellenkanarios.com/001O/001O/001O/IndependenceDefinition
Independence evolves in the following manner:
Events A and B are independent if P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B).
A finite collection of events are independent if P(A_{k_1} \cap \cdots \cap A_{k_j}) = P(A_{k_1}) \cdots P(A_{k_j})
A infinite collection of sets is defined to be independent if every finite subcollection is independent.
Classes \mathscr {A}_1, \ldots , \mathscr {A}_n are independent if for any events A_1 \in \mathscr {A}_1, \ldots , A_n \in \mathscr {A}_n, A_1, \ldots , A_n are independent. 20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001P/001P/001P/Independent \pi -systems generate independent \sigma -algebrasTheorem
If \mathscr {A}_1, \ldots , \mathscr {A}_n are independent and each \mathscr {A}_i is a \pi -system then \sigma (\mathscr {A}_1), \ldots , \sigma (\mathscr {A}_n) are independent.
Proof. We have sequence of independent classes and want to show that their respective \sigma -algebras are also independent. The idea is to take the class of events B_1 that are independent with \mathscr {A}_2, \ldots , \mathscr {A}_n, and show that \sigma (\mathscr {A}_1) is a subset of this class. To do this, we use Dynkin's \pi \text {-}\lambda by noticing that this class is a \lambda -system that contains the \pi -system \mathscr {A}_1.
Meaning in english: In the book, they give the example of even and odd numbered coin tosses. We want to know when a new event only depends on even or odd coin tosses. Concretely, we know that even and odd coin tosses are independent, and we want to know if, for example, the outcome of every fourth coin toss is independent of odd tosses. We can do this rigorously by showing that this event is in the \sigma -algebra of even coin tosses. Then by Theorem 5.7, we know that this event is also independent of odd coin tosses. 20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001R/001R/001R/Array MethodTheorem
Suppose that the array
\begin {array}{ccc} A_{11} & A_{12} & \cdots \\ A_{21} & A_{22} & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \end {array}
of events is independent. If \mathscr {F}_1 is the \sigma -algebra generated by the first row then \mathscr {F}_1, \mathscr {F}_2, \ldots are independent.
Example. Can write events of flip i as H_i such that the following array satisfies the above
\begin {array}{ccc} H_{2} & H_{4} & \cdots \\ H_{1} & H_3 & \cdots \\ \end {array}
Then row 1 and row 2 are independent from above. Therefore, we have shown that even and odd numbered flips' \sigma -field's are independent.
Kellen Kanarios20241030Notebook: Probability and measure › Subfields
Remark. Intuition of subfields are spaces with partial information. My best memory of this coming up in probability was conditional expectation. Esssentially, we need to consider the subfield of events conditioned on some event that already occured.
20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001S/001S/001S/\mathscr {A}-equivalent pointsDefinition
We say that points \omega and \omega ' are \mathscr {A} equivalent if for every A \in \mathscr {A}, I_A(\omega ) = I_A(\omega ')
From this definition, we can conclude an observer with the information of \sigma (\mathscr {A}) has only the information of the equivalence class but cannot identify a specific point from this class.
Kellen Kanarios20241030Notebook: Probability and measure › The Borel-Cantelli Lemmas 20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001Q/001Q/001Q/Borel-Cantelli OneTheorem
If \sum _{n}P(A_n) converges, then P(\lim \sup _n A_n) = 0.
Proof. Recall that \lim \sup _n A_n \subset \bigcup _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k. Therefore, P(\lim \sup _n A_n) \leq P\left (\bigcup _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k\right ) \leq \sum _{k = n}^{\infty } P(A_k) Taking n \to \infty yields the desired result.
20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001T/001T/001T/Borel-Cantelli TwoTheorem
If \{A_n\} is an independent sequence of events and \sum _{n}P(A_n) diverges, then P(\lim \sup _n A_n) = 1.
Proof. Consider the complement. By independence, we have P(\bigcap _{k = n}^{n + m} A_k^c) = \prod _{k = n}^{n + m}(1 - P(A_k)) \leq \exp (-\sum _{k = n}^{n + m} P(A_k)), where the last inequality comes from 1 - x \leq e^{-x}. Since the series diverges, taking m \to \infty yields the desired result.
Kellen Kanarios20241030Notebook: Probability and measure › Zero-one Law 20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001U/001U/001U/Tail \sigma -fieldDefinition
For a sequence A_1, A_2, \ldots of events in a probability space (\Omega , \mathscr {F}, P) consider the \sigma -fields \sigma (A_n, A_{n+1}, \ldots ) then their intersection \mathscr {T} = \bigcap _{n = 1}^{\infty } \sigma (A_n, A_{n+1}, \ldots ) is the tail \sigma -field associated with the sequence \{A_n\}.
Example. The \lim \sup _n A_n and \lim \inf _n A_n are both tail events.
20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001V/001V/001V/Kolmogorov's Zero-one LawTheorem
If A_1, A_2, \ldots is an independent sequence of events, then for each event A in the Tail \sigma -field, P(A) is either 0 or 1.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Consider the function X = \sum _{i} x_i I_{A_i} If A_i are finite partition of \Omega into \mathscr {F}-sets, then inverse image is at most countable union of the A_i's, which is an \mathscr {F}-set. Therefore, this function is measurable.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X_1, \ldots , X_n be Simple Random Variable's
The \sigma -field \sigma (X_1, \ldots , X_n) consists of the sets \left [(X_1, \ldots , X_n) \in H\right ] = \left [\omega \mid (X_1(\omega ), \ldots , X_n(\omega )) \in H\right ] for H \subset \mathbb {R}^n.
A simple random variable Y is measurable \sigma (X_1, \ldots , X_n) if and only if Y = f(X_1, \ldots , X_n) for some f: \mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R}.

The \sigma -field generated by a Simple Random Variable is the smallest \sigma -field such that the Simple Random Variable is measurable. For a sequence of Simple Random Variable's, the \sigma -field generated by them is the smallest \sigma -field, such that each of the Simple Random Variable's is measurable.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Probability and measure › Convergence of Random Variables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Probability and measure › Convergence of Random Variables

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
In probability, rather than standard convergence, we actually can consider convergence with high probability or almost surely (should come up later). Formally, we care about measuring the probability of the event \{\omega \mid \lim _n X_n(\omega ) \to X(\omega )\}. The first question we must ask is whether this set is measurable. They do not really cover this in the textbook, but it is an important trick that comes up a lot.
Proof. We consider the complement. Note that the sequence of random variables not converging can be written as \forall \epsilon , \exists n such that |X_n(\omega ) - X(\omega )| \geq \epsilon . For a fixed \epsilon , this is the \lim \sup of the sequence A_n \coloneqq [|X_n - X| \geq \epsilon ]. We know that \lim \sup of measurable sequence is measurable because it is a countable intersection of countable unions. Therefore, the last thing we need is to show for all \epsilon . This can be done just using rationals because \mathbb {Q} is dense in \mathbb {R}. Then we have a countable union of countable sets as desired.
20241228https://kellenkanarios.com/001Z/001Z/001Z/Almost Sure Convergence
A Simple Random Variable converges almost surely if P(X_n \to X) = 1 20241228https://kellenkanarios.com/0020/0020/0020/Convergence in Probability
A Simple Random Variable converges in probability if for every \epsilon \lim _{n \to \infty } P(|X_n - X| > \epsilon ) = 0
Remark. By Theorem 5.5, almost sure convergence \implies convergence in probability. Converse is NOT true.
Example. Can make array of rows, where the nth row consists of n intervals that evenly divide [0,1]. Then P(A_n) \to 0 but [A_n \text { i.o}] = [0,1]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A sequence of random variables X_1, X_2, \ldots is independent if the classes \sigma (X_1), \sigma (X_2), \ldots are independent.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Probability and measure › Existence of Independent Sequences

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Probability and measure › Existence of Independent Sequences

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The distribution of a simple random variable X is the probability measure \mu defined for all subsets A of the line by \mu (A) = P[X \in A]

fr:title.text: Existence of independent sequences with prescribed distributions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Existence of independent sequences with prescribed distributions

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \{\mu _n\} be a sequence of probability measures on the class of all subsets of the line, each having finite support. There exists some probability space (\Omega , \mathscr {F}, P) an independent sequence \{X_n\} of Simple Random Variable's such that X_n has distribution \mu _n.

Guess for english translation: I think what this is saying is that rather than try to define a probability space for a random variable, we can instead just pick probability measures and know that the Simple Random Variable come for free? i.e. (from the book) we can just define a trivial space \Omega = \{1, 0\} and probability measure P(1) = p, P(0) = 1 - p and know that we can get a Bernoulli random variable? In this case X(1) = 1, X(0) = 0 such that P(X = 1) = p and P(X = 0) = 1 - p?
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. In the text, they say that the underlying probability space does not really matter. Basically, all calculations are determined by the distribution in range space. So I guess that is also what this theorem is getting at?

fr:title.text: Notebook: Probability and measure › Expected Value

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Probability and measure › Expected Value

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Simple Random Variable of the form X = \sum _{i} x_i I_{A_i} is assigned expected value as \mathbb {E}[X] = \mathbb {E}\left [\sum _i x_i I_{A_i} \right ] = \sum _i x_i P(A_i) More generally, if f(X) = \sum _i f(x_i) I_{A_i} then \mathbb {E}\left [f(X)\right ] = \sum _i f(x_i) P(A_i) = \sum _x f(x) P[X = x] A useful identity is as follows: \mathbb {E}[X] = \int _0^{\infty } P[X \geq x] \mathrm {d}x
We define the kth moment of X as \mathbb {E}[X^k] = \sum _y y P[X^k = y]
If \exists K such that X_n(\omega ) \leq K for every \omega and n then the X_n are uniformly bounded.
If \{X_n\} is uniformly bounded, and if X = \lim _n X_n with probability 1, then \mathbb {E}[X] = \lim _n \mathbb {E}[X]
If \phi is a convex function on the range of X then \phi (\mathbb {E}[X]) \leq \mathbb {E}[\phi (X)]

Suppose that \frac {1}{p} + \frac {1}{q} = 1, \quad p > 1,\ q > 1 Then Holder's inequality is \mathbb {E}[XY] \leq \left (\mathbb {E}[|X|^p]\right )^{1/p} \cdot \left (\mathbb {E}[|Y|^q]\right )^{1/q}
For each subset A of \Omega , P^{*}(A)=\mathrm {inf}\,\sum _{n}P(A_{n}), where A \subset \bigcup _{n = 1}^{\infty } A_n.
A is P^*-measurable if for every E \subset \Omega , we have that P^*(A \cap E) + P^*(A^c \cap E) = P^*(E)

For these notes, I will attempt to refresh my knowledge of measure-theoretic probability along with learning stochastic processes for the first time. I will be following Probability and Stochastics by Cinlar. Unfortunately, there are no solutions to Exercises, but it is by far the best written book I have found.
The worked exercises are collected separately in Billingsley Probability and Measure: Exercises. What stays here is the conceptual development.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
20241120https://kellenkanarios.com/000S/000S/000S/Additivity of IntervalsTheorem
We have the following properties
If \bigcup _{k}I_{k}\subset I, and the I_{k} are disjoint, then \sum _{k}|I_{k}|\leq |I|.
If I\subset \bigcup _{k}I_{k} (the I_{k} need not be disjoint), then |I|\leq \sum _{k}|I_{k}|.
If I=\bigcup _{k}I_{k}, and the I_{k} are disjoint, then |I|=\sum _{k}|I_{k}|.
Remark. The important takeaway from Additivity of Intervals is that extending (2) from finite to countable unions requires Heine-Borel theorem, illustrating that this extension is non-trivial. This will be important to remember as we continue to extend the Lebesgue measure to \sigma -algebras.
This section aims to motivate why we need to approach probability from a measure theoretic point of view. As a concrete example, Dyadic Intervals sets up the dyadic-interval representation of an infinite coin toss, and Exercise 1.1 makes precise why no discrete probability space can carry that sequence. 20241114https://kellenkanarios.com/000L/000L/000L/Dyadic IntervalsExample
Represent coin toss as dyadic expansion consider the set\left [\omega :\ d_{i}(\omega )=u_{i},\,i=1,\ldots ,n \right ]=\Big (\sum _{i=1}^{n} \frac {u_{i}}{2^{i}},\ \sum _{i=1}^{n}\frac {u_{i}}{2^{i}} + \frac {1}{2^{n}}\Big ).
More stuff I think my main takeaway from this is that infinite sequences break discrete probability because each discrete event will have probability zero. This is not actually a problem because in an infinite sequence the occurence of any such sequence "feasibly" has zero probability. However, we still want to reason about probabilistic things like the convergence of the average of the sequence. Since discrete probability is broken, we cannot even use it for these things. This is why we need to use measure theory to allow for zero probability sequences, but still provide information about these more "general" events. Who knows if this intuition is right though?

html:ul.html:li: After a bit more digging, the main culprit here is the countable subadditivity condition of measure.

In a discrete probability space, the entire space is at most countable. Therefore, if each individual event has zero probability then the countable union i.e. the whole space will have zero probability.
In measure theoretic probability, we are able to accumulate non-zero probability mass from an uncountable union of zero measure events.
I guess the canonical example of this is that each point on a line has length 0, but the line itself (an uncountable union of points) has non-zero length.
In the dyadic intervals example, each event of an arbitrary discrete probability space can be identified with a point on the line of probability zero.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It seems, in this section, we aim to answer why certain decisions were made regarding what can be analyzed. For example,
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Why do we only allow consider countable unions in the definition of a \sigma -field?
Answer. Because this is what god intended.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If \mathscr {F} is a \sigma -field in \Omega and P is a probability measure on \mathscr {F}, the triple (\Omega ,\mathscr {F},P) is called a probability measure space, or simply a probability space. A support of P is any \mathscr {F}-set A for which P(A)=1.

fr:title.text: Continuity and countable subadditivity of a probability measure

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Continuity and countable subadditivity of a probability measure

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let P be a probability measure on a field \mathscr {F}.
Continuity from below: If A_{n} and A lie in \mathscr {F} and \mathscr {A}_{n}\uparrow A, then P(A_{n})\uparrow P(A).
Continuity from above: If A_{n} and A lie in \mathscr {F} and A_{n}\downarrow A, then P(A_{n})\downarrow P(A).
Countable subadditivity: If A_{1},A_{2},\ldots and \bigcup _{k=1}^{\infty }A_{k} lie in \mathscr {F} (the A_{k} need not be disjoint), then
P\left (\bigcup _{k=1}^{\infty }A_{k}\right )\leq \sum _{k=1}^{\infty }P(A_{k})

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Here we will see how we can define a probability measure on an algebra \mathscr {F}_0 and get a unique extension to the \sigma -field \mathscr {F} = \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0). I guess the key here is uniqueness? 20241121https://kellenkanarios.com/000T/000T/000T/Outer MeasureDefinition
For each subset A of \Omega , P^{*}(A)=\mathrm {inf}\,\sum _{n}P(A_{n}), where A \subset \bigcup _{n = 1}^{\infty } A_n.
Remark. Taking P_*(A) = 1 - P^*(A^c) gives the inner measure. This is just analogously approximating from the inside.
Intuition is that the sets that are "measurable" should be sets that can be arbitrarily approximated from the inside and outside, or equivalently P^*(A) + P^*(A^c) = 1
My intution for this is that if a set is not arbitrarily well approximated by countable other sets from our algebra then we cannot hope to get an accurate measure from our measure defined on only the algebra.
The countableness is just from the definition of the \sigma -field.
Then we can use P^* as our measure. To do this, we will need to show that P^* is countable additive as one might expect. The rest of the properties of a Probability Measure are trivial.
Proof.
Suppose that A \subset \bigcup _{n = 1}^{\infty } A_n. By the definition of Outer Measure, we can find some B_{nk}, such that A_n \subset \bigcup _{k = 1}^{\infty } B_{nk} where \sum _{k} P(B_{nk}) < P^*(A_n) + \epsilon 2^{-k} Now take C_n = \bigcup _{k} B_{nk}, so that \bigcup _{n} A_n \subset \bigcup _{n} C_n and P^*\left (\bigcup _n A_n\right ) < \sum _{n} P(C_n) = \sum _{n} \sum _{k} P(B_{nk}) < \sum _{n} P^*(A_n) + \epsilon Since \bigcup _{n} A_n = A, we have achieved the desired result.
It turns out that for some reason only enforcing A and its complement to sum to 1 is not restrictive enough? From this, we end up with the final condition
20241121https://kellenkanarios.com/000U/000U/000U/P^*-measurableDefinition
A is P^*-measurable if for every E \subset \Omega , we have that P^*(A \cap E) + P^*(A^c \cap E) = P^*(E)
You then only need to show two things:
The class of P^*-measurable sets contains \mathscr {F_0},
The class of P^*-measurable sets is a \sigma -field. From these, we can conclude that P^* is at least defined on a \sigma -field containing \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0). Restricting P^* to \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0), then yields the desired extension.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
We now want to show that the extension we developed in the previous section is actually unique. The first question we have is what do we mean by unique? To answer this, we need to remember what we have done so far.
We have a Probability Measure P, defined on an algebra \mathscr {F}_0.
We then extended P to \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0) via P^*, so that for every A \in \mathscr {F}_0 we have P(A) = P^*(A) and P^* is defined on all of \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0) From this, our only restriction is that P^*(A) = P(A) for A \in \mathscr {F}_0. Therefore, uniqueness here means that if P^*(A) = Q(A) for some Q and every A \in \mathscr {F}_0. Then P^*(A) = Q(A) for every A \in \sigma (\mathscr {F}_0).
A class \mathcal {P} of subsets of \Omega is a \pi -system if it is closed under the formation of finite intersections: A,B\in \mathcal {P} \implies A\cap B\in \mathcal {P}.
I was a bit unsure that a \lambda -system is not a \sigma -field, but the author provided an example that I will walk through to understand the distinction
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Consider a four point space \Omega = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}. We can define a \lambda -system by taking \mathscr {L} = \emptyset , \Omega , and the six two point sets. The union of any two disjoint two point sets will be \Omega . However, a non-disjoint union of two disjoint sets can result in a three point set, which is not in \mathscr {L}. These non-disjoint unions would be covered in a \sigma -field.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: To remedy this disconnect, we have the following lemma:

fr:title.text: A \pi -system that is also a \lambda -system is a \sigma -field

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A \pi -system that is also a \lambda -system is a \sigma -field

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A class that is both a \pi -system and a \lambda -system is a \sigma -field.
In our example, we could take the intersection of say \{x_1, x_2\} and \{x_2, x_3\} to get \{x_2\}. We could then take a disjoint union of \{x_1, x_3\} and \{x_2\} to get \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}.
f \mathscr {P} is a \pi -system and \mathscr {L} is a \lambda -system, then \mathscr {P}\subset \mathscr {L} implies \sigma (\mathscr {P})\subset \mathscr {L}.
Now we will see how this allows us to prove our desired result i.e. the uniqueness of our extension of a probability on an algebra via the outer measure.

Suppose that P_{1} and P_{2} are probability measures on \sigma (\mathscr {P}), where \mathscr {P} is a \pi -system. If P_{1} and P_{2} agree on \mathscr {P}, then they agree on \sigma (\mathscr {P}).
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Proof. Take \mathscr {L} to be the class of sets such that A \in \mathscr {L} \implies P_1(A) = P_2(A). Since \mathscr {P} is a \pi -system, from Theorem 9, it is sufficient to show that \mathscr {L} is a \lambda -system. Since \Omega \in \mathscr {P}, \Omega \in \mathscr {L}. If A \in \mathscr {L}, then P_1(A^c) = 1 - P_1(A) = 1 - P_2(A) = P_2(A^c). Therefore, A^c \in \mathscr {L}. Finally, if A_1, A_2, \ldots disjoint then
P_1\left (\bigcup _{n = 1} A_n\right ) = \sum _{n = 1} P_1(A_n) = \sum _{n = 1}P_2 (A_n) = P_2\left (\bigcup _{n = 1}A_n\right )
Thus, \mathscr {L} is a \lambda -system. From Theorem 9, \sigma (\mathscr {P}) \subset \mathscr {L} and P_1 and P_2 agree on all of \sigma (\mathscr {P}).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An (important?) note is that this works due to our definition of a Probability Measure. Namely, countable additivity is really a condition on countable disjoint unions, meaning that the \sigma -field induced by a Probability Measure does not make full use of the generality allowed under arbitrary countable unions because it must satisfy countable additivity.
For completeness and future use, we introduce here the notion of monotone classes and Halmo's useful theorem.
If \mathscr {F}_{0} is a field and \mathscr {A} is a monotone class, then \mathscr {F}_{0}\subset \mathscr {A} implies \sigma (\mathscr {F}_{0})\subset \mathscr {A}.
Also for completeness, we introduce completeness lol.
A probability measure (\Omega , \mathcal {F}, P) is complete if for A \subset B if P(B) = 0 then P(A) = 0.
You might be wondering when this is not the case because we have subadditivity. However, it is not the case that P(A) > 0 but instead that A is not measurable. As an example,

If we consider [0,1], then we can partition [0, 1] into equivalent classes as x \sim y if x - y = r for some r \in \mathbb {Q} \cap [0, 1]. Denote each of these as V_{r}. By the Axiom of Choice, we can choose one representative from each V_r. We call the set of these representatives V. Therefore, we can write [0, 1] as a countable union via [0,1] = \bigcup _{r \in \mathbb {Q}} V + r. For the Lebesgue measure \lambda , each \lambda (V + r) = \lambda (V). Since each V + r is disjoint, we have \lambda \left (\bigcup _{r \in \mathbb {Q}} V + r\right ) = \sum _{r \in \mathbb {Q}} \lambda (V) If \lambda (V) = 0, then \lambda ([0,1]) \leq \lambda (V) = 0 which is a contradiction. Otherwise \lambda ([0,1]) = \sum _{r \in \mathbb {Q}} k = \infty for some k \in \mathbb {R}. Thus, V is not Lebesgue measurable.
Exercise 3.2 makes the characterization sharp: a set is P^*-measurable iff its outer and inner measures agree.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The results of this section concern infinite sequences of events in a probability space. They will be illustrated by examples in the unit interval. By this will always be meant the triple (\Omega , \mathscr {F}, P) for which \Omega is (0,1), \mathscr {F} is the \sigma -field \mathscr {B} of Borel sets there, and P(A) is for A in \mathscr {F} the Lebesgue measure \lambda (A) of A. 2024122https://kellenkanarios.com/001J/001J/001J/Conditional ProbabilityDefinition
P(A | B) = \frac {P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} 2024122https://kellenkanarios.com/001I/001I/001I/Chain Rule of ProbabilityProposition
P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A)P(B | A)P(C | B \cap A) 20241226https://kellenkanarios.com/001M/001M/001M/limsup of setsDefinition
For a sequence A_1, A_2, \ldots of sets, we define \lim \sup _{n} A_n = \bigcap _{n = 1}^{\infty } \bigcup _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k In english, an element \omega is in \lim \sup _{n} A_{n} if \omega is in A_{n} infinitely often.
20241226https://kellenkanarios.com/001N/001N/001N/liminf of setsDefinition
For a sequence A_1, A_2, \ldots of sets, we define \lim \inf _{n} A_n = \bigcup _{n = 1}^{\infty } \bigcap _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k In english, an element \omega is in \lim \inf _{n} A_{n} if \omega is in all but finitely many A_{n}.
2024122https://kellenkanarios.com/001K/001K/001K/Fatou's lemma for eventsTheorem
For each sequence \{A_{n}\},
P\left (\lim \inf _{n}A_{n}\right )\leq \lim _{n}\inf _{n}P\left (\,A_{n}\right ) \leq \lim _{n}\sup _{n}P\left (\,A_{n}\right )\leq P\left (\lim _{n}\sup _{n}A_{n}\right ).
Proof. We define B_n \coloneqq \bigcup _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k and B_n as C_n = \bigcap _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k. Note that \{B_n\} is a monotonically decreasing sequence to \lim \sup _n A_n, and C_n monotonically increases to \lim \inf _{n} A_n. Therefore, we can apply continuity from below / above such that
\begin {align*} \inf _n P(A_n) \geq P(C_n) \iff \lim _n \inf _n P(A_n) &\geq \lim _n P(C_n) \\ &= P(\lim _n C_n) \\ &= P(\lim \inf _n A_n) \end {align*}
Similarly,
\begin {align*} \sup _n P(A_n) \leq P(B_n) \iff \lim _n \sup _n P(A_n) &\leq \lim _n P(B_n) \\ &= P(\lim _n B_n) \\ &= P(\lim \sup _n A_n) \end {align*}
20241226https://kellenkanarios.com/001O/001O/001O/IndependenceDefinition
Independence evolves in the following manner:
Events A and B are independent if P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B).
A finite collection of events are independent if P(A_{k_1} \cap \cdots \cap A_{k_j}) = P(A_{k_1}) \cdots P(A_{k_j})
A infinite collection of sets is defined to be independent if every finite subcollection is independent.
Classes \mathscr {A}_1, \ldots , \mathscr {A}_n are independent if for any events A_1 \in \mathscr {A}_1, \ldots , A_n \in \mathscr {A}_n, A_1, \ldots , A_n are independent. 20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001P/001P/001P/Independent \pi -systems generate independent \sigma -algebrasTheorem
If \mathscr {A}_1, \ldots , \mathscr {A}_n are independent and each \mathscr {A}_i is a \pi -system then \sigma (\mathscr {A}_1), \ldots , \sigma (\mathscr {A}_n) are independent.
Proof. We have sequence of independent classes and want to show that their respective \sigma -algebras are also independent. The idea is to take the class of events B_1 that are independent with \mathscr {A}_2, \ldots , \mathscr {A}_n, and show that \sigma (\mathscr {A}_1) is a subset of this class. To do this, we use Dynkin's \pi \text {-}\lambda by noticing that this class is a \lambda -system that contains the \pi -system \mathscr {A}_1.
Meaning in english: In the book, they give the example of even and odd numbered coin tosses. We want to know when a new event only depends on even or odd coin tosses. Concretely, we know that even and odd coin tosses are independent, and we want to know if, for example, the outcome of every fourth coin toss is independent of odd tosses. We can do this rigorously by showing that this event is in the \sigma -algebra of even coin tosses. Then by Theorem 5.7, we know that this event is also independent of odd coin tosses. 20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001R/001R/001R/Array MethodTheorem
Suppose that the array
\begin {array}{ccc} A_{11} & A_{12} & \cdots \\ A_{21} & A_{22} & \cdots \\ \vdots & \vdots & \end {array}
of events is independent. If \mathscr {F}_1 is the \sigma -algebra generated by the first row then \mathscr {F}_1, \mathscr {F}_2, \ldots are independent.
Example. Can write events of flip i as H_i such that the following array satisfies the above
\begin {array}{ccc} H_{2} & H_{4} & \cdots \\ H_{1} & H_3 & \cdots \\ \end {array}
Then row 1 and row 2 are independent from above. Therefore, we have shown that even and odd numbered flips' \sigma -field's are independent.
Kellen Kanarios20241030Notebook: Probability and measure › Subfields
Remark. Intuition of subfields are spaces with partial information. My best memory of this coming up in probability was conditional expectation. Esssentially, we need to consider the subfield of events conditioned on some event that already occured.
20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001S/001S/001S/\mathscr {A}-equivalent pointsDefinition
We say that points \omega and \omega ' are \mathscr {A} equivalent if for every A \in \mathscr {A}, I_A(\omega ) = I_A(\omega ')
From this definition, we can conclude an observer with the information of \sigma (\mathscr {A}) has only the information of the equivalence class but cannot identify a specific point from this class.
Kellen Kanarios20241030Notebook: Probability and measure › The Borel-Cantelli Lemmas 20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001Q/001Q/001Q/Borel-Cantelli OneTheorem
If \sum _{n}P(A_n) converges, then P(\lim \sup _n A_n) = 0.
Proof. Recall that \lim \sup _n A_n \subset \bigcup _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k. Therefore, P(\lim \sup _n A_n) \leq P\left (\bigcup _{k = n}^{\infty } A_k\right ) \leq \sum _{k = n}^{\infty } P(A_k) Taking n \to \infty yields the desired result.
20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001T/001T/001T/Borel-Cantelli TwoTheorem
If \{A_n\} is an independent sequence of events and \sum _{n}P(A_n) diverges, then P(\lim \sup _n A_n) = 1.
Proof. Consider the complement. By independence, we have P(\bigcap _{k = n}^{n + m} A_k^c) = \prod _{k = n}^{n + m}(1 - P(A_k)) \leq \exp (-\sum _{k = n}^{n + m} P(A_k)), where the last inequality comes from 1 - x \leq e^{-x}. Since the series diverges, taking m \to \infty yields the desired result.
Kellen Kanarios20241030Notebook: Probability and measure › Zero-one Law 20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001U/001U/001U/Tail \sigma -fieldDefinition
For a sequence A_1, A_2, \ldots of events in a probability space (\Omega , \mathscr {F}, P) consider the \sigma -fields \sigma (A_n, A_{n+1}, \ldots ) then their intersection \mathscr {T} = \bigcap _{n = 1}^{\infty } \sigma (A_n, A_{n+1}, \ldots ) is the tail \sigma -field associated with the sequence \{A_n\}.
Example. The \lim \sup _n A_n and \lim \inf _n A_n are both tail events.
20241227https://kellenkanarios.com/001V/001V/001V/Kolmogorov's Zero-one LawTheorem
If A_1, A_2, \ldots is an independent sequence of events, then for each event A in the Tail \sigma -field, P(A) is either 0 or 1.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Consider the function X = \sum _{i} x_i I_{A_i} If A_i are finite partition of \Omega into \mathscr {F}-sets, then inverse image is at most countable union of the A_i's, which is an \mathscr {F}-set. Therefore, this function is measurable.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X_1, \ldots , X_n be Simple Random Variable's
The \sigma -field \sigma (X_1, \ldots , X_n) consists of the sets \left [(X_1, \ldots , X_n) \in H\right ] = \left [\omega \mid (X_1(\omega ), \ldots , X_n(\omega )) \in H\right ] for H \subset \mathbb {R}^n.
A simple random variable Y is measurable \sigma (X_1, \ldots , X_n) if and only if Y = f(X_1, \ldots , X_n) for some f: \mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R}.

The \sigma -field generated by a Simple Random Variable is the smallest \sigma -field such that the Simple Random Variable is measurable. For a sequence of Simple Random Variable's, the \sigma -field generated by them is the smallest \sigma -field, such that each of the Simple Random Variable's is measurable.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Probability and measure › Convergence of Random Variables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Probability and measure › Convergence of Random Variables

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
In probability, rather than standard convergence, we actually can consider convergence with high probability or almost surely (should come up later). Formally, we care about measuring the probability of the event \{\omega \mid \lim _n X_n(\omega ) \to X(\omega )\}. The first question we must ask is whether this set is measurable. They do not really cover this in the textbook, but it is an important trick that comes up a lot.
Proof. We consider the complement. Note that the sequence of random variables not converging can be written as \forall \epsilon , \exists n such that |X_n(\omega ) - X(\omega )| \geq \epsilon . For a fixed \epsilon , this is the \lim \sup of the sequence A_n \coloneqq [|X_n - X| \geq \epsilon ]. We know that \lim \sup of measurable sequence is measurable because it is a countable intersection of countable unions. Therefore, the last thing we need is to show for all \epsilon . This can be done just using rationals because \mathbb {Q} is dense in \mathbb {R}. Then we have a countable union of countable sets as desired.
20241228https://kellenkanarios.com/001Z/001Z/001Z/Almost Sure Convergence
A Simple Random Variable converges almost surely if P(X_n \to X) = 1 20241228https://kellenkanarios.com/0020/0020/0020/Convergence in Probability
A Simple Random Variable converges in probability if for every \epsilon \lim _{n \to \infty } P(|X_n - X| > \epsilon ) = 0
Remark. By Theorem 5.5, almost sure convergence \implies convergence in probability. Converse is NOT true.
Example. Can make array of rows, where the nth row consists of n intervals that evenly divide [0,1]. Then P(A_n) \to 0 but [A_n \text { i.o}] = [0,1]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A sequence of random variables X_1, X_2, \ldots is independent if the classes \sigma (X_1), \sigma (X_2), \ldots are independent.

fr:title.text: Notebook: Probability and measure › Existence of Independent Sequences

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Probability and measure › Existence of Independent Sequences

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The distribution of a simple random variable X is the probability measure \mu defined for all subsets A of the line by \mu (A) = P[X \in A]

fr:title.text: Existence of independent sequences with prescribed distributions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Existence of independent sequences with prescribed distributions

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \{\mu _n\} be a sequence of probability measures on the class of all subsets of the line, each having finite support. There exists some probability space (\Omega , \mathscr {F}, P) an independent sequence \{X_n\} of Simple Random Variable's such that X_n has distribution \mu _n.

Guess for english translation: I think what this is saying is that rather than try to define a probability space for a random variable, we can instead just pick probability measures and know that the Simple Random Variable come for free? i.e. (from the book) we can just define a trivial space \Omega = \{1, 0\} and probability measure P(1) = p, P(0) = 1 - p and know that we can get a Bernoulli random variable? In this case X(1) = 1, X(0) = 0 such that P(X = 1) = p and P(X = 0) = 1 - p?
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. In the text, they say that the underlying probability space does not really matter. Basically, all calculations are determined by the distribution in range space. So I guess that is also what this theorem is getting at?

fr:title.text: Notebook: Probability and measure › Expected Value

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Notebook: Probability and measure › Expected Value

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Simple Random Variable of the form X = \sum _{i} x_i I_{A_i} is assigned expected value as \mathbb {E}[X] = \mathbb {E}\left [\sum _i x_i I_{A_i} \right ] = \sum _i x_i P(A_i) More generally, if f(X) = \sum _i f(x_i) I_{A_i} then \mathbb {E}\left [f(X)\right ] = \sum _i f(x_i) P(A_i) = \sum _x f(x) P[X = x] A useful identity is as follows: \mathbb {E}[X] = \int _0^{\infty } P[X \geq x] \mathrm {d}x
We define the kth moment of X as \mathbb {E}[X^k] = \sum _y y P[X^k = y]
If \exists K such that X_n(\omega ) \leq K for every \omega and n then the X_n are uniformly bounded.
If \{X_n\} is uniformly bounded, and if X = \lim _n X_n with probability 1, then \mathbb {E}[X] = \lim _n \mathbb {E}[X]
If \phi is a convex function on the range of X then \phi (\mathbb {E}[X]) \leq \mathbb {E}[\phi (X)]

Suppose that \frac {1}{p} + \frac {1}{q} = 1, \quad p > 1,\ q > 1 Then Holder's inequality is \mathbb {E}[XY] \leq \left (\mathbb {E}[|X|^p]\right )^{1/p} \cdot \left (\mathbb {E}[|Y|^q]\right )^{1/q}
